title: 字母袖扣谋杀案
summary:
Hercule Poirot’s investigation begins at "The Happy Coffee Shop" in London, where he encounters a distraught woman named Jane (also known as Jenny or Jean). She appears to be fleeing from someone and expresses a terrifying conviction that her death is inevitable and necessary for justice. She begs Poirot, a retired policeman, to swear that if she dies, he will not instruct his police contacts to investigate the killer. Following her sudden disappearance, Poirot learns from the cafe staff that Jane previously worked as a maid and often spoke with an unnatural, noble manner.

The investigation intensifies when Poirot’s companion, Carter, reports three chillingly organized murders at the Brawnsham Hotel. The victims—Harriet Sipper, Edith Gransbury, and Richard Nigges—were found in their locked rooms, meticulously arranged on the floor with their arms at their sides and palms down. Most strikingly, each victim had a gold cufflink engraved with the initials "PIJ" (or "PJI") placed in their mouths. Poirot realizes that Jane’s earlier warning—to "not let them talk"—was a specific instruction to prevent investigators from opening the victims' mouths and discovering the cufflinks.

Poirot and Carter investigate the hotel, noting several inconsistencies. While the victims were found in similar positions, Richard Nigges's room was unique: his window was open, his cufflink was pushed deep into his throat, and a key was found hidden behind a loose fireplace tile, suggesting he had locked the door from the inside and exited through the window. Poirot also notes that the victims had all checked in on the same day and requested room service at the same time. Through further inquiry, it is revealed that Nigges had pre-paid for the rooms and car rentals for the others, suggesting he may have been an unwitting accomplice in bringing the victims to the hotel.

The investigation expands to the village of Glebeholme, where Carter discovers a connection to a tragedy from sixteen years prior. In 1913, a local clergyman, Patrick James Ives, and his wife, Frances, died by suicide after being accused of spiritualist fraud and improper conduct. The accusations were fueled by malicious rumors spread by a maid named Jenny Hobbs and supported by Harriet Sipper, Ada Gransbury, and Richard Nigges. The village's condemnation had led to the couple's deaths, leaving "invisible wounds" on the survivors.

Returning to London, Poirot identifies the woman seen fleeing the hotel as the portrait painter Nancy Duane. While Nancy has an alibi provided by the Wallace family, Poirot suspects a deeper conspiracy. He discovers that the "impersonator" seen at the hotel—a man claiming to be Nigges—was actually Samuel Kid, a skilled mimic who helped facilitate the crime. Poirot realizes the murders were not a series of random killings, but a staged event.

Poirot eventually confronts Jane Hobbs, revealing that she and Nancy Duane had conspired to manipulate the situation. The truth is a complex web of guilt and survival: Richard Nigges, consumed by remorse for his role in the Ives' deaths, had proposed a suicide pact to Harriet, Ada, and Jenny to achieve religious atonement. However, the plan was subverted. Jenny Hobbs, driven by a desire to survive and a long-standing jealousy of Nancy Duane, manipulated the group. She convinced Nigges that Nancy was evil, while simultaneously working with Nancy to frame the others for the deaths.

The "murders" were actually a combination of planned suicides and killings. Jenny Hobbs killed Harriet Sipper and Richard Nigges to ensure the plan's progression and to protect herself. The victims were poisoned with cyanide earlier than the police believed, and the "impersonation" of Nigges at the hotel was a ruse to create a false timeline of life. The meticulously arranged bodies and the placement of the "PIJ" cufflinks were intended to make the deaths appear to be the work of a single, ritualistic killer, thereby directing suspicion toward Nancy Duane.

In a final, violent turn, Jane Hobbs stabs Mrs. Dunn during the confrontation. Poirot concludes the case by explaining that the entire sequence—the "alphabet cufflink murders"—was a manufactured tragedy designed to serve as a twisted form of justice and a mechanism for framing an innocent woman. Poirot admits to Carter that he used certain deceptions during the investigation to force him to utilize his own deductive reasoning.
